speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 17, 1995. The survivor is Cissie Nathan, born Rabbie. My name is Renee Firestone. We are in Rancho Mirage, California, in the United States, and the language is English. It is November 17, 1995. I will be speaking to Cissie Nathan. S-I-- C-I-S-S-I-E N-A-T-H-A-N, born Rabbie, R-A-B-B-I-E. My name is Renee Firestone. R-E-N-E-E F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. We are in Rancho Mirage, California, in the United States, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mrs. Nathan. We thank you for letting us come into your home and take you back into your past. Would you please start with telling your name, spelling it for us, and tell us where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. My name is Cissie Nathan. I was born in London on the 8th of November, 1905.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your name for us? Spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cissie Nathan? My name is spelled C-I-S-S-I-E N-A-T-H-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your born name, your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Cissie Rabbie, R-A-B-B-I-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us a little bit about your father? What was his name? What did he do for a living? When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad was born in Holland. His name was Moses Maurice Rabbie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: His profession, he was a agent, I believe, for a piano firm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your relationship to your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was the eldest of five. I'm-- my father was always very proud of me because I'd been to school and had a scholarship. I'd been learning music, piano. He bought me a piano. And I won two certificates at the College of Music in London. And-- but then my father, god rest his soul, died of it when influenza first came to England. He was 38 years old. He would've been 39 in January. He died on my birthday-- birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about how it happened, and what do you remember of that time when he passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he passed away, I was the eldest, naturally, and 14 on that day. I had to sit shiva there we were with my mother. After that, I had to give up school and had to try and earn some money to help my mother. There was money left, but it wasn't sufficient. There were five of us, and my grandmother lived with us in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to night school with a friend and learned shorthand and typing. I got a position in an office in the city of London and used to travel up from where we lived every day back and forth. My mother, god rest her soul, was a dressmaker by profession, so she took it up again.  But-- Tell us-- tell us your mother's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Rebecca Rabbie, of course. So she took it-- the dressmaking up again. But at that time they were wearing a lot of beading on their dresses, and she could not do that. So when I came home from work, I used to bead the dresses till about 1 o'clock in the morning. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 14 on that day, yes, that my father died. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's maiden name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Clark. Rebecca Clark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very close to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were all very close family. My grandmother, my mother's mother, lived with us. We were all very-- she outlived my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you started to work at the age of 14.  Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You started to work--

speaker: interviewee
text: At 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: --almost at the age of 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember of any fun time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that age, not really, you know. As I got older, naturally, there was fun. And I had girlfriends, and we used to go dancing when I was older. And you know, we-- we had a close-knit family, so there was always something, because my dad's two brothers were married, lived in the same road where we lived, and all the children. We were all very close, very happy together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many siblings did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many--

speaker: interviewer
text: Brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many aunts?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry. I had two brothers and two sisters. Yes. One-- one sister is still alive. She's in London in a home. She has--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister who's still alive, her name is Lily. Yeah. My eldest brother's name was Sam, Samuel. My other sister's name-- she was the one next to me-- her name was Anne. And my youngest brother's name was Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sister that is still alive, where is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister that's still alive is in a home in London because she has Parkinson's disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you see her last?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw her this year. I go and see her every time I'm over here, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about how you were growing up with the-- with your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we all grew up-- we were all very-- well, together. We-- we were always close with all of them. But it was a close-knit family with all the uncles and aunts and close-- my dad being the eldest. You see, my dad's father died when he was young. His mother-- so he came to England when he was 18, brought his mother with him. And his two brothers came to England, too. They were younger than my father. The youngest brother, my Uncle Lou, had to go to school. But the other brother was 14, and he went and did some work, which I don't remember what it was. I think it was somewhere in a cigar factory, but that's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you growing up? Where were you raised?

speaker: interviewee
text: In England. I-- I was growing up in England until 1928. My brother said to me, my eldest brother, would you like to go to the Olympics to see the swimming? And I was always interested, so I said yes, I'll go with you. So I went with him to Amsterdam to see the Olympic swimming. And there I met my son's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Isaac. They called him [? Ise. ?] And his name was Rabbie because he was a cousin. Never met. Never knew him. He was strange to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you grew up in England where, in what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The town where you grew up.

speaker: interviewee
text: London.

speaker: interviewer
text: In London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born and bred in London. We all were. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came to Holland and you met your first husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, for the Games. And then, I think it was a couple of months later, he came over to England. And we corresponded for quite a while. And then he came over again and we got engaged. And then-- that was, I think, 1929. And in 1930, I went to Holland to get married. My grandmother came with me at that time because I went in May and stayed with my grand-- had a son there. Stayed there, and then my mother and the family came over. We got married in August 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1930. I was 24 when that happened, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you settle after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Amsterdam. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: First I-- first I lived in the Jewish quarter where my in-laws lived. And then we moved to the southern quarter of Amsterdam, a whole new area. Yeah. I-- and my husband being a diamond worker, there was a recession then.  The recession started. So I went to work in a office in Amsterdam, English, doing English correspondence. After that, [BACKGROUND NOISE] I became pregnant and left work. And Maurice, my son was born, Maurice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was born on the 15th of March, 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I-- may I go back for a-- for a minute? When you were growing up in London, were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not strict Orthodox, but they were religious. We kept everything. yeah. Or [INAUDIBLE] my grandmother was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe, let's say, a Friday night in your house in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually, not always, but usually the brothers used to come with their family. If any relatives from Holland came over, it was always in our house that all the family came, all the family. I remember the First World War, '14-'18 war. When there was an air raid, a lot of people used to go underground or to the station. But my dad-- we had a very large dining room table-- turned the table upside down and put all their little ones in it. The men used to play cards. The women used to knit, make the coffee, whatever. I remember that vividly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate Passover? Did you have Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had-- yes. Everything was changed, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your-- did your mother light candles on Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything we did. Everything that we were supposed to do. My grandmother was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you light candles on Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here. Every-- yes. Oh, yes, sure. And my son does the brochos. He does it all. [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to-- to Amsterdam now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son was born, and you stopped working then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. After my son and my daughter were born, I didn't work anymore. No, I didn't have to work anymore. But I ran the house well. We had a-- they call it a condo here. We had a flat, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the life in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: The life in Amsterdam was good. I had a brother-in-law married with a daughter who went to Auschwitz. My in-laws went to Auschwitz. And I had a sister-in-law married with a little son who also went-- went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But let's talk about before they went to Auschwitz. What-- what kind of life did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we always-- we always used to see each other, always used to meet, always holidays or birthdays, always together. And there was family of my father-in-law there, and we met up. We used to meet up with all the family. We had quite a good life there. Really good life. Yes. And the food in Amsterdam is better than anywhere. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember at all any anti-Semitism in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't as far as we knew. We never experienced it at all, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Dutch people resent foreigners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't find that. No, no, no. And they all spoke English in Holland because they learn it at school. So there was no problem. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In London. You went to school in London, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Quite near where we lived. It was Malmesbury Road School in Bow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how much schooling did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: How much--

speaker: interviewer
text: Schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, till I was 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which meant? You went to elementary?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a primary school first. And then at 11, you do the exams and you get a-- and I got a scholarship and then went on from that. And I should have stayed on but I couldn't. My mom had to come before the board and tell them why I had to leave, which I did, as I said before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the rest of the children continue school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They-- they-- yes. They all continued school until they were old enough to go out to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to Holland, when you decided to remain in Holland--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did your mother remain in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course. My mother remained with the other-- my brothers and sisters. They were born-- all born in England. Of course. And my grandmother was there. No, we all-- they all remained. My-- when they were old enough, they went out to work the same as I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- how did it feel to be separated from such a close-knit family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very strange at first. But we-- but one or the other always used to visit. Yeah. My mother used to come. And I used to go to England with the children. And then my late husband, my husband, Maurice's father, would come from a Friday till a Sunday, something like that, for weekends, when business permitted, of course, when work permitted. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find living in Holland different from living in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really. The only thing is, I wasn't very-- I didn't know a lot of hot Dutch. My-- my late mother-in-law took me shopping, and you know, I learned more. And then one day she said to me, now you're on your own. Now you've got to do it yourself. I had the two children then. So there was the two children in the pram shopping. We got on very well. But after a while, when the children were born, I had a lady to help me once a week, you know, with the cleaning and that was my [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what age did the-- your children start school in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children started school, I think it was five. Five years old. They went to a Jewish kindergarten before that that I have some pictures of. And just for a couple of hours in the morning, from 9:00 till 12:00, something like that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your husband also kept a Jewish home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Similar to your parents' home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Maybe not quite as strict. But Jewish home, yeah. My father and all used to light Hanukkah candles and yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the children really grew up in a very Jewish atmosphere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in a Jewish community, yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any Zionist organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time, no. Not at all. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? Well, one girlfriend I met, Leah, she died three years ago. She was in concentration camp too. She was with me for a lot of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet Leah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Leah through a-- a lady cousin by marriage. I met her, and then we were always very-- she worked after the war as a beautician. And I was working as a millinery designer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you two do before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland? Nothing. Only the family, and I-- I didn't work. Only the period before the children were born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your friend, did she work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she worked. She was a beautician. She worked, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any non-Jewish friends in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: London friends?

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. We only had Jewish friends. Yeah. But my mother was a sister of mercy. That's what they used to call her. The road we lived in was every religion, every religion. And if somebody was sick, everybody helped, no matter what their religion was. And you'd go to school with their children, you know. They would-- I'll tell you a secret. Sometimes on Simchat Torah, when they give children sweets, we used to take a couple of the others in with us sometimes. Only two. They were-- but my mom was a very good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how come in Holland you didn't have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody did at that time. None of us did. Only girls that I worked with in the office. It was a big office, every nationality. But not friends to go out with or anything. No. Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had my home and my children. Didn't have the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the girls and-- and the men in the office relate to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were all right. They were-- in the office we all got on very well together. We had a very big office. Yeah. There was one German girl, non-Jewish. She was very, very friendly. Yeah. She was very nice. But I never went out with her, but just friendly in the office, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you in the office, all of the Dutch people and yourselves, did you know that there is a danger coming? Did you know that Hitler is in--

speaker: interviewee
text: We in Holland all thought that Holland would be neutral like it was in the First World War. That's what everybody thought. My mother, god rest her soul, came over in August 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came over to Holland, and she said, the boys-- that's my brothers-- want you all to come home because there's going to be trouble. And my husband quite assured they wouldn't come through Holland. But apart from that, so he-- he-- my mother said, if you won't come-- because I couldn't leave him-- if you won't come, let me take the children. And Maurice's father said, where I go, my children go. Wouldn't let them go. So that's why we were caught in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe your mother when she said that there would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. My two brothers were in the British forces. My eldest brother was a sick bay officer in the Royal Navy, and my youngest brother was in the Army. My youngest brother was taken prisoner of war in Singapore. He was in the Japanese prison camp. But my eldest brother came home on compassionate leave when my mother was-- died.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your mother die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- next to where we lived in England, in London, a schoolmaster lived. And my mother had got a envelope-- telegram from the British forces to say that my brother was missing in action. So that's why my eldest brother was home on compassionate leave. Then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we were in-- when we got transported from Amsterdam, we-- we were in one of the last groups to go to Westerbork, a camp in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get to it. Let's-- let's see where your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know when your mother passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- yeah, now. I knew up to the war, when. But that was later. That was much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she die, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died on the 4th of December, 1944, the same day and the same time that they took my husband, Maurice's father, when they separated us. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- when did you first realize that there is real danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: We only realized there was a real danger when they-- the Germans went into Belgium and then came into Holland. As a matter of fact, I was-- the day they entered Amsterdam, I was standing at the corner of the road where we lived with the doctor that birthed my two children and saw them march in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think? What went through your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can imagine how I felt. You can imagine how we all felt. This doctor was a young Jewish doctor who went underground. They changed his name and sent him in another hospital a long way away. So he survived. I saw him after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from the street that day when they arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to where we lived, of course. The children were there. We lived. And then my husband, being one of the diamond workers in Amsterdam, they were essential to the Germans because they needed the Germans to buy munitions. So we had a special-- what did they call it? A spare? That if we were picked up, if I was picked up with the children, or my husband, they wouldn't take us.
